322·6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. 82-053 
Gentlemen: 
Thank you for your letter requesting advice from this 
office as to your duties under the Pp}itical Reform Act, Gov-
ernment Code Sections 81000, et ~.-
The following advice is 
provided to you pursuant to Section 83ll4(b). 
My understanding of the situation is as follows. 
Both of 
you are employees of the Contra Costa County Health Services 
Department. 
In his capacity as Executive Director of the Contra 
Costa Health Plan, Mr. Kaplan recommended to the County on 
September 8, 1981, that a contract for consulting services with 
Henry Leavitt be approved. 
Mr. Leavitt is Vice President of 
Health Sector Management, Inc., a private consulting firm which 
specializes in studies on and analyses for prepaid health manage-
ment systems. 
The contract was approved and performed. 
Subsequently Health Sector Management contacted the two of 
you regarding the possibility of your participating with them 
in a consulting contract for the City and County of San Francisco. 
The contract calls for a feasibility study of organized health 
system options for San Francisco General Hospital. 
Both of you 
have since participated in some consultation sessions with 
Health Sector Management, Inc., for this contract with San Francisco. 
You anticipate receiving approximately $5,000 each in total 
compensation for this contract. 
All statutory references are to t 
unless otherwise noted. 
Government Code 


Arnold Sterne Le 
,M.D. 
Robert H. Kaplan 
April 15, 1982 
Page Two 
You asked whether any conflicts of interest could arise 
out of this situation. 
First, I would like to point out that this office renders 
advice only on the Political Reform Act. 
There may be other 
laws which are relevant to this matter. 
The County Counselor 
Attorney General's Office can assist you with the interpretation 
of other conflict of interest laws. 
The Act does not prohibit these consulting activities; 
however, it does prohibit you as public officials from participating 
in any governmental decision in which you have a financial 
interest. 
Section 87100. 
An official has a financial interest 
in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its 
effect on the public generally, on any business entity or real 
property in which the official has an investment of $1,000 or 
more, or on any source of income of $250 or more to the official 
within the previous 12 months. 
Section 87103. 
So long as Health Sector Management, Inc., is a source of 
income of over $250 to you, you will have to disqualify yourselves 
from all decisions which will foreseeabl¥/have a material financial 
effect on Health Sector Management, Inc.-
These decisions 
would clearly include decisions as to whether Health Sector 
Management, Inc., will receive contracts for consulting services 
from the County. 
I trust that this discussion has been helpful. 
If I can 
be further assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916) 
322 -5901.

(copy enclosed), further defines what constitutes a "material 
financial effect" on a business entity.

sections.question: Whether any conflicts of interest could arise from the situation where two county health services employees are participating as consultants with Health Sector Management, Inc. on a San Francisco contract, given that they previously recommended approval of a contract with the same firm for the County.
sections.conclusion: The Act does not prohibit these consulting activities; however, it does prohibit you as public officials from participating in any governmental decision in which you have a financial interest. Section 87100. So long as Health Sector Management, Inc., is a source of income of over $250 to you, you will have to disqualify yourselves from all decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Health Sector Management, Inc. These decisions would clearly include decisions as to whether Health Sector Management, Inc., will receive contracts for consulting services from the County.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the requesters, as county employees, would face conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act by participating in consulting work with Health Sector Management, Inc., a firm they previously recommended for a county contract.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that while the Political Reform Act does not prohibit the consulting activities, the requesters must disqualify themselves from any governmental decisions that would have a material financial effect on Health Sector Management, Inc., specifically including decisions regarding whether that firm receives county contracts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (dated March 9, 1982) and the FPPC response letter (dated April 15, 1982). Both sections were analyzed. The advice letter is clearly labeled with standard FPPC response format from Counsel Diane Marohisburn. Statutory references to Government Code Sections 81000, 83114(b), 87100, and 87103 are cited.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether any conflicts of interest could arise from the situation where two county health services employees are participating as consultants with Health Sector Management, Inc. on a San Francisco contract, given that they previously recommended approval of a contract with the same firm for the County.

CONCLUSION: The Act does not prohibit these consulting activities; however, it does prohibit you as public officials from participating in any governmental decision in which you have a financial interest. Section 87100. So long as Health Sector Management, Inc., is a source of income of over $250 to you, you will have to disqualify yourselves from all decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Health Sector Management, Inc. These decisions would clearly include decisions as to whether Health Sector Management, Inc., will receive contracts for consulting services from the County.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether two Contra Costa County health services employees have conflicts of interest by participating as consultants with Health Sector Management, Inc. on a San Francisco project, when they had previously recommended the firm for county work. The FPPC concludes the activities are not prohibited but require disqualification from certain county decisions.
